OK

You have a time machine and can travel to 2100, you have select wines from the 2010-2015 years to drink. Knowing what you know now which wines do you think will be drinkable and or still good.

Alban Reva/Lorraine/Seymour
Saxum Bone Rock
Monte Bello
Dunn Howell Mountain
Turley Petite sirah
Bedrock Heritage Zin ( maybe not but id love to try it )

Any other ideas

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Matt Parham:

I love California wine and support the industry more than most, but have a hard time believing much will be alive in 2100. How many 80+ plus year old Bordeaux are still alive and kicking? Dunn would be my best guess for California.

I agree. We’re talking about wines 85-90 years old. Very few in the world can get there, even from Bordeaux or Burgundy. There’s no question that nothing being produced today is all that similar to the famous really old California wines that have gone that distance. I might be more confident if there were sites in CA with track records for producing great, ageworthy styles of Tempranillo or Nebbiolo. Really, my only guess is Dunn Howell Mountain from a good vintage (maybe '10 or '13), and I’m not at all confident about that. Several very ageworthy wines mentioned will likely be great in 40 years, but we’re talking about over twice as long.

posts.name: Sean Malloy
posts.cooked: Alan: Good question, but I think it overshoots the mark with 2100. Like others have said, my guess would be basically none will make it that far. That’s just a really long time for anything. There’s lots of good answers in the guesses above if you made it 2050 or 2060, though.

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
Hi Folks,

Really a fun idea to throw around. There are lots of Burgundies and Bordeaux that make it that long- for instance, good grand crus from 1937 are still brilliant- Drouhin Bonnes-Mares a fairly recent case in point- and 1928 Bordeaux still have decades and decades of life ahead of them. So, the time frame is not unreachable, but it might be more realistic to rein it in a bit to say 2075 to make the scenario a bit more easy to project. The one thing to keep in mind about those old Burgundies and Bordeaux that are still cruising along so beautifully at very advanced ages is that they started out life far lower in alcohol than what we typically see today in either of those regions, not to mention California, and it is the octane factor that gives me the most pause about projecting out similar longevity for more contemporary wines. Last week, I had the good fortune to drink 1961 and 1959 Magdelaine, alongside 1959 Trotanoy and all of those wines were absolutely singing and still right in the heart of their apogees of peak maturity. All three will clearly cruise along at least another thirty years and will probably make their century mark still with some vigor. But the 1961 was probably under twelve percent and the '59s would have been no more than 12.5 percent, if that, and so we are talking wines that really do not have too many modern equivalents in today’s world of wine. The question is whether or not wines at 13 to 14.5 percent will be able to age as gracefully as these oldies (or older wines that have continued to cruise along beautifully). I think it is pretty safe to say that anything north of 14.5 percent is just not going to show the same potential longevity.

The key thing to keep in mind is that these wines that have aged so long like the Burgundies from the decades of the thirties or forties, or the Bordeaux from the twenties, have aged so long based on their exquisite balances, not their tannic structures- with the exception of the outlier 1928 Bordeaux vintage. It is not their superior concentration or tannic backbone that gave these legendary old wines their longevity, it was impeccable balance (this is particularly true of a vintage like 1961 in Bordeaux, which may well be the most precisely and elegantly balanced vintage of the 20th century on the Gironde) that gave them the chassis to revel in the passage of time, rather than fear it. And these are the attributes that we should look at to pick our candidates for 2100 or 2075 from California.

With that perspective as my foundation, I would nominate wines such as Ridge Monte Bello (still usually under 13% ABV in most vintages), Philip Togni, Mayacamas and Mount Eden as my cabernet candidates. Honorable mentions should go to Heitz and Cathy Corison as well, but I am just not sure if we can look for this length of potential longevity from fruit from the Valley floor here, and that is why my candidates are all mountain cabernets. That said, of course, the Heitz wines I have had from the 1960s are all still really fine and not yet showing signs of decline, but they were also a bit lower in octane back in those days. If we reined in the far limit for aging to fifty years, I would not hesitate to add both of these producers to my list, but eighty-five years might be a stretch. I just do not have enough recent experience with the Dunn or Diamond Creek wines to know if they will go that far, but their wines from the 1980s might make it to 2100! Dominus also made really long-lived wines back in the day, but their more current releases are much higher in octane than their first releases and I just cannot see them giong deep into the second half of the 21st century for that reason. With pinot noir from California, I am not sure if we will see quite this length of longevity, but my long-distance runners with this varietal would inlcude Kalin Cellars, Rhys, Mount Eden and Joseph Swan amongst others as possibly having the requisite balance out of the blocks to make really old bones (even if they do not make it all the way to 2100). With Kalin Cellars, if they were still producing cabernet sauvignon, I would certainly nominate them for eighty-five years of potential life in bottle.

Beyond these two varietals, maybe the syrahs from Steve Edmunds, Rhys and Wells Guthrie might go deep as well, based on their lovely balances, but this seems to be pretty uncharted territory and they may not make it deeper than fifty years. I do not think we will see any zinfandels go the length of time that the legendary old zins from Simi did in their day, as the octanes these days seem to dictate a shorter period of peak maturity for this grape. I know there are some outliers of older zinfandel citings at 14+% ABV that have aged well (Sarah’s recent note on a higher octane '78 Ridge Zinfandel would be exhibit A) , but my experience has generally been that zinfandels over 14% may survive over several decades in bottle, but they do not drink as well at age thirty or forty as they did at age ten. On the other hand, a wine like the Ridge '74 Lytton Springs zinfandel has improved along the way and is a fantastic drink today and has done a lot more with its time in bottle than simply survive.

All the Best,

John

Hi John,

I am on board with most of what you say, but your remarks about Dominus raise some interesting points. While Dominus is certainly weighing in a bit riper than it used to (1991 is labelled 13.5, 1994 is a bit higher than that, both still going very strong, though maybe not actually as complex as e.g. ‘92), the grapes are much better protected from sunburn and raisining, and sorting for these thing is more rigorous. So the more recent Dominus vintages do not have the aromatic dimension of torrefaction one finds in the sunny vintages of the early days (pretty much the last millenium). What is more, in terms of phenolics, I would imagine that the tannins of more recent vintages might actually have more vitality (i.e. less field oxidation) than the earlier, more roasted wines. This is just a speculation, but it’s an interesting question. Of course, the reason I cited Togni but not Dominus in my post above was because I would imagine Dominus’ life expectancy to be more in the region of 50 years, so we are not in disagreement on that front.

My sense, for what it’s worth, is that the Heitz wines these days are really picked pretty ripe, but that is just based on a few tastings. They also go through malo now, whereas there are rumours that they didn’t routinely do so back in the day.

Regarding Ridge, in some ways Monte Bello may be the best candidate of all for serious longevity, as the pHs there are really very low indeed for red wines in California, or even in contemporary France—we are talking about 3.2-3.4 as a typical range as I recall! In fact, that would be considered low for quite a few Californian Chardonnays…

posts.name: Barry P
posts.cooked: I think any of the Abreus will easily go 50-75 years and probably longer. I frankly don’t agree on the Monte Bello. Love the wine, and in particular some of the early to mid 90s vintages, but I just don’t think the stuffing is there for the really, really long haul. Maybe Mayacamas, as John suggests. Maybe the Dunn Howell. But Abreu has the structure and the fruit to go longer than anything else out there.

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Hi John,

I am on board with most of what you say, but your remarks about Dominus raise some interesting points. While Dominus is certainly weighing in a bit riper than it used to (1991 is labelled 13.5, 1994 is a bit higher than that, both still going very strong, though maybe not actually as complex as e.g. ‘92), the grapes are much better protected from sunburn and raisining, and sorting for these thing is more rigorous. So the more recent Dominus vintages do not have the aromatic dimension of torrefaction one finds in the sunny vintages of the early days (pretty much the last millenium). What is more, in terms of phenolics, I would imagine that the tannins of more recent vintages might actually have more vitality (i.e. less field oxidation) than the earlier, more roasted wines. This is just a speculation, but it’s an interesting question. Of course, the reason I cited Togni but not Dominus in my post above was because I would imagine Dominus’ life expectancy to be more in the region of 50 years, so we are not in disagreement on that front.

My sense, for what it’s worth, is that the Heitz wines these days are really picked pretty ripe, but that is just based on a few tastings. They also go through malo now, whereas there are rumours that they didn’t routinely do so back in the day.

Regarding Ridge, in some ways Monte Bello may be the best candidate of all for serious longevity, as the pHs there are really very low indeed for red wines in California, or even in contemporary France—we are talking about 3.2-3.4 as a typical range as I recall! In fact, that would be considered low for quite a few Californian Chardonnays…

Hi William,

My only concern with Dominus these days going deep enough to make it to 2100 is the higher octane level the wines are made with today, which certainly produces a far more accessible profile out of the blocks than was the case in the 1980s and early 1990s. Whether or not this wil compromise potential longevity on the other end of its evolutionary cycle is certainly speculative at this point, but my gut instincts are that it will. The more sophisticated tannins and fresher fruit aspects that they certainly have today will be obviated by the alcohol is my guess, but it is only a guess. BTW, fully agree with you on Monte Bello.

The Hetiz wines did not go through malo in the past and continue not to do so today. They are probably a touch riper than was the case in the '60s, '70s and '80s, but Joe liked to pick later and his wines were always north of 13% when many of his colleagues would be coming in sub-thirteen octane. David Heitz has been making the wines here since 1974 and he suggested when we chatted about this on my last visit out to the winery that the alcohols were probably a bit higher than what was on the label back in the day, so that the difference is probably no more than half a point higher today (where the wines are labeled at 14.5 instead of 13.5%). To my palate they are probably at least that half point higher today (and I would be delighted to see it back down in the 13.5% range), which I would attribute partly to global warming and partly to changing perceptions of tannin ripeness etc. But, alcohol levels aside, I would think that David is pretty much making the cabernets at Heitz Cellar much the same as he ever did. They continue to start out life in those large, old foudres for their first year before being racked into French casks to finish their elevage, and they continue not to go through malo and are still bottled on the late side. I have some of the younger vintages of cabernet buried in my cellar to see how they treat me in my old age, and would love it if they reached the same level of complexity as my Heitz wines from the '60s and '70s that are still drinking so beautifully when I treat myself to one out of the cellar.

All the Best,

John

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Thanks for the Heitz insights, John. I will have to head down the road to taste there some time soon. I am pretty familiar with their wines from the '70s, but my experience with recent vintages is only episodic by comparison.

William Kelley:

My sense, for what it’s worth, is that the Heitz wines these days are really picked pretty ripe, but that is just based on a few tastings. They also go through malo now, whereas there are rumours that they didn’t routinely do so back in the day.

Do you have a source for this? A winery rep told me just a couple of years ago that they block MLF on all of their Cabernets.

This article says the same:

rarewineco.com

Heitz Martha's Vineyard Cabernet Sauvignon - Rare Wine Co. - Rare Wine Co.

Nearly a half century after it became the first great single-vineyard California Cabernet, Martha’s Vineyard still mesmerizes with its astonishing bouquet and its warm, velvety texture.

This one does too, on page 10.
https://www.heitzcellar.com/pdf/Celebrating%20Fifty%20Years%20of%20Napa%20Valley%20Excellence.pdf

participants.name: Glenn L e v i n e
